title: Block algebra for morphing circuits
abstract: Morphing circuits are a new paradigm for quantum error correction that relaxes hardware requirements. We present four constructions for CNOT-based CSS morphing circuits with explicit qubit connectivity degrees. All four constructions are specified in block algebra notation, with entries in algebras generated by permutation matrices. The first three are obtained by rewriting existing surface- and color-code morphing circuits; the fourth is a new three-round construction modeled on the 6.6.6 color code. The surface-code construction recovers the morphing circuit of Ref. [ST25] for two-block group algebra codes. Numerical search then instantiates these permutation matrices using regular representations of finite groups.   [ST25] M. H. Shaw and B. M. Terhal, Phys. Rev. Lett. 134(9), 090602 (2025).
summary: This paper develops a block algebra formalism to describe morphing circuits used in quantum error correction. It presents four explicit constructions that encode established codes like the surface and color codes into this general algebraic structure. This advances the theoretical understanding of fault-tolerant computation by providing systematic circuit design tools.
main_problem: The research focuses on developing an algebraic framework, using block algebra notation, to describe 'morphing circuits' for quantum error correction.
main_result: Four explicit constructions (I-IV) are presented for CNOT-based CSS morphing circuits, allowing known codes like the surface and color codes to be represented in this general algebraic form.
method: The authors employ an algebraic viewpoint by viewing contraction schedules as Gaussian elimination patterns over block stabilizer matrices, generalizing translation-invariant code structures into polynomial sums.
model_or_system: The model is Quantum Error Correction (QEC) using CSS codes (e.g., surface and color codes). The circuits are represented abstractly via block algebra notation involving permutation matrices acting on syndrome measurements.
key_observables: Mid-cycle code stabilizer matrices ($H_X, H_Z$), qubit connectivity degree, and the resulting logical error rate after simulation.
important_parameters: The number of contraction rounds ($J$) and the specific commutation relations between polynomial parameters (e.g., $PR = QS$).
main_assumptions: It is assumed that known translation-invariant codes can be mapped to polynomial sums within this general block algebra framework.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate morphing circuits, comparing surface code derivations to the new algebraic constructions (e.g., Fig. 3), and show site layouts for various color codes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of morphing circuits algebraically, presents four specific constructions (I-IV) tailored to different known codes (surface/color), details the mathematical constraints required for each construction, and concludes with simulation setup notes.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, systematic algebraic toolset for designing fault-tolerant quantum computation protocols by relaxing hardware connectivity requirements in QEC circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
